text.title: event-driven-architecture Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for event-driven-architecture. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Publishers emit events to topics without knowledge of subscribers. Subscribers register independently and process at their own pace. The broker decouples the two.
rule.rationale: Producer-consumer awareness creates coupling. When the producer must know about each consumer, adding a new consumer requires a producer change — defeating the extensibility benefit of EDA.

rule.statement: Events in EDA are integration events (cross-service); events in event sourcing are domain events (within a service). Do not conflate them. A domain event may or may not be published as an integration event.
rule.rationale: Publishing every internal domain event externally creates tight coupling between a service's internal model and its consumers. Select which domain events become integration contracts.

rule.statement: In EDA, read models are built by consuming events from the write side. The projection is eventually consistent — design UI and APIs to handle this (pending state, polling, websocket updates).
rule.rationale: Assuming immediate consistency in an eventually consistent system causes UI bugs where newly submitted data does not appear in the next page load.

rule.statement: Every saga step must have a written compensating action before the saga is deployed. Compensation runs in reverse order when any step fails.
rule.rationale: A deployed saga without compensations is a data corruption waiting to happen. Step 1 side effects (reservation, charge) become permanent when step 2 fails with no rollback path.

rule.statement: Use orchestration-based sagas when more than 3-4 services participate. Choreography with many services creates spaghetti event flows with no central place to observe state.
rule.rationale: Choreography distributes the saga state machine across services; debugging a failed saga requires tracing events across all participants. Orchestration keeps state in one place.

rule.statement: Use Kafka when: high-volume event streaming (>100K msg/s), event replay required, strong per-partition ordering needed, or integrating with Spark/Flink pipelines.
rule.rationale: Kafka is a distributed commit log with configurable retention. Its pull-based model and partitioned topics provide the highest throughput and replay capability. Trade-off: operational complexity (ZooKeeper/KRaft, partition management).

rule.statement: Use RabbitMQ when: complex routing (exchanges, bindings, headers), multiple protocol support (AMQP, MQTT, STOMP), or smaller-scale messaging with very low latency is needed.
rule.rationale: RabbitMQ's push-based AMQP model provides sub-millisecond latency at small scale and flexible routing. Messages are removed after consumption — replay requires a separate store.

rule.statement: Use SQS+SNS when: fully managed is required, workloads are AWS-native, scale is variable (serverless, bursty), and simplicity outweighs flexibility.
rule.rationale: Zero operational overhead at the cost of limited ordering guarantees (FIFO queues exist but are limited), at-least-once delivery only, and AWS vendor lock-in.

rule.statement: Name events in past tense describing a completed business outcome: OrderPlaced, PaymentReceived, UserRegistered. Never use imperative names (CreateOrder) or CRUD names (OrderUpdated).
rule.rationale: Past tense signals immutability and distinguishes events (facts that happened) from commands (intentions). CRUD names like OrderUpdated lose intent — what specifically changed?

rule.statement: Use a reverse-domain type hierarchy: {company}.{bounded-context}.{aggregate}.{event} — e.g., com.example.orders.order.placed.
rule.rationale: Namespaced types prevent collisions in shared brokers and make event routing rules explicit and grep-able.

rule.statement: Use CloudEvents v1.0 format for all integration events. Required attributes: specversion, id, source, type. Add datacontenttype, subject, time as optional attributes.
rule.rationale: CloudEvents is a CNCF graduated standard supported by all major cloud providers and brokers. Standardized envelopes enable tooling (routers, transformers, monitors) to work across all event types.

rule.statement: Only make additive, backward-compatible changes to existing event schemas: add optional fields, never remove required fields, always provide default values for new fields.
rule.rationale: Events in the log are immutable but consumers must continue to deserialize old events. Removing a field or making a formerly-optional field required breaks replay of historical events.

rule.statement: Enforce schema compatibility with a schema registry (Confluent Schema Registry, Apicurio, Buf BSR). Fail PRs that introduce non-additive changes.
rule.rationale: Without automated enforcement, non-additive schema changes slip through review and silently break consumers at runtime. The registry is the contract boundary.

rule.statement: Design all consumers for at-least-once delivery. Exactly-once is expensive and often unavailable. At-most-once is only acceptable for metrics or logs where data loss is tolerable.
rule.rationale: Most brokers (Kafka, RabbitMQ, SQS) guarantee at-least-once under failure scenarios. Assuming exactly-once without transactional producers + idempotent consumers leads to double-processing bugs in production.

rule.statement: Every consumer must be idempotent: processing the same event_id twice must produce the same result as processing it once. Use a dedup table, upsert semantics, or natural idempotency.
rule.rationale: Brokers redeliver on consumer failure or network hiccup. Without idempotency, duplicate delivery causes double-charges, double-emails, double-inventory-decrements.

rule.statement: Publish events reliably via the outbox pattern: write the event row and the business row in the same DB transaction; a separate worker reads and publishes to the broker.
rule.rationale: Publishing to the broker inside the business transaction risks a committed event that is never published (broker call fails after DB commit) or an event published for a rolled-back transaction.
